summary: Validated cross-layer record. The Session Manager Executive worker-thread pair now has a clean baseline export on Win25H2Clean, a bounded boot-time ETL extract that proves runtime access to Session Manager\Executive, exact current-build ntoskrnl string hits, preserved Ghidra fallback artifacts, a supporting ReactOS semantic hypothesis, and a tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up that produced exact RegQueryValue hits for both AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads under a concurrent I/O and process-burst trigger. That is enough for Class A within this project even though the lane remains research-only and non-actionable in the app.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: The tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330 produced exact RegQueryValue hits for both AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads under the concurrent burst trigger, with shell health preserved before and after the run.
validation_proof.notes: This proof anchors the Executive worker-thread pair to a real clean-baseline export, a bounded boot-time runtime extract, current-build ntoskrnl string and Ghidra fallback evidence, and a tools-hardened lightweight ETW runtime lane that produced exact live reads for both values. It is strong enough for Class A while still leaving app actionability off.

setting.name: Executive Additional Worker Threads
setting.casual_explanation: These values appear to control extra executive worker threads for internal critical and delayed work queues. They are low-level kernel-adjacent knobs and are being tracked as research only.
setting.why_people_change_it: Advanced researchers may inspect these values when studying executive worker pool sizing, boot-time work scheduling, or internal queue behavior under Session Manager Executive.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: The pair is boot-relevant and kernel-adjacent. Cross-layer evidence is now strong enough for Class A in this project, but the values remain low-level research surfaces rather than safe shipped presets.
setting.professional_notes: The tracked values are HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Executive\AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads. A clean Win25H2Clean export confirmed both values at 0 on the baseline. The first string pass found exact Unicode hits for both names in current-build ntoskrnl.exe and no exact hits in smss.exe. The improved Ghidra fallback then preserved forced-boundary reviewable artifacts at 140c62b88 and 140c62bb8. A bounded host-side ETL extract from the successful boot trace also proved that System (PID 4) touches Session Manager\Executive during early boot and actively queries plus sets the adjacent UuidSequenceNumber value in the same path. Procmon boot-log and the first shell-safe post-boot stress trigger lanes still returned zero exact hits, which kept the lane below Class A. The follow-up changed the runtime method instead of the classification rule: on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330, a lightweight ETW lane with a concurrent I/O and process-burst trigger finally produced exact RegQueryValue hits for both AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads while shell health stayed clean before and after the run. A supporting ReactOS source pass also found both value names in the registry data table and found matching internal variables in ntoskrnl work-queue code where the additional worker-thread counts are clamped and then added to the base delayed and critical worker counts. That ReactOS lead stays supporting-only because the registry table maps both names to DummyData and ReactOS is not current-build Windows. Within this project, the new exact runtime read plus the existing static and structural layers are enough for Class A while still leaving the lane research-only and non-actionable.

allowed_values.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The clean VM baseline currently exposes both Executive worker-thread values as 0.

allowed_values.label: Undocumented custom DWORD pair
allowed_values.meaning: The evidence so far only proves that the pair exists, is referenced in current-build ntoskrnl, and still avoids an exact-value Procmon boot-log or shell-safe stress-trigger hit on the current baseline. A safe non-default profile is not validated yet.

targets.notes: Keep the pair together. The lane is stronger when AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads are treated as one candidate surface.

app_current_implementation.provider_source: not currently shipped in the app
app_current_implementation.notes: There is no current RegProbe provider or UI mapping for the Executive worker-thread pair. The lane is still research-only intake.

windows_defaults.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current Win25H2Clean baseline with the Session Manager Executive key populated

states.rationale: Leave the currently observed Executive worker-thread pair unchanged until a stronger semantic map exists.

windows_defaults.notes: UuidSequenceNumber was also present in the same baseline export, but it remains a separate candidate lane.

recommended_profiles.label: Observed baseline

states.rationale: Keep the pair at the current observed baseline while evidence gathering continues.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the current baseline without inventing a non-default worker-thread profile.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a shipped tweak or a claimed performance recommendation.
tradeoffs: Preserves the current observed Executive worker-thread pair.
Keeps the lane documentation-only until a stronger semantic map exists.

recommended_profiles.label: Research-only verified pair

states.rationale: Keep the pair as a research-only verified surface rather than a shipped preset.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting or comparing the verified Executive worker-thread pair without shipping it as a preset.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a documented end-user recommendation.
tradeoffs: Keeps the lane verified in research without exposing it as a one-click tweak.
Still does not provide a validated non-default preset for end users.

evidence.title: Tools-hardened lightweight ETW runtime follow-up for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: A tools-hardened lightweight ETW lane on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330 used a concurrent I/O and process-burst trigger and produced exact RegQueryValue hits for both AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads, while shell health stayed clean before and after the run.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Session Manager Executive baseline export
evidence.summary: A clean VM baseline export confirmed AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads = 0 and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads = 0 under Session Manager\Executive, with UuidSequenceNumber also present as adjacent context.

evidence.title: Current-build ntoskrnl Ghidra fallback for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: The current-build ntoskrnl.exe pass found exact string hits for AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads and preserved forced-boundary fallback artifacts at 140c62b88 and 140c62bb8.

evidence.title: Kernel power static probe summary for existing Session Manager candidates
evidence.summary: The first static probe confirmed exact Unicode hits in ntoskrnl.exe for the Executive worker-thread pair and no exact hits in smss.exe.

evidence.title: Kernel power next-gate intake summary
evidence.summary: The 2026-03-28 intake review kept the Executive worker-thread pair active as the second next-gate lane after the stronger watchdog pair.

evidence.title: Bounded host-side ETL review for Session Manager Executive
evidence.summary: A bounded host-side ETL extract from the successful boot trace proved early-boot access to Session Manager\Executive by System (PID 4) and captured adjacent UuidSequenceNumber query and set activity. The exact worker-thread pair names still did not appear in the bounded runtime payload.

evidence.title: ETW-specific keyword review for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: An exact-keyword review over the bounded Executive ETW extract still surfaced only Session Manager Executive path activity plus adjacent UuidSequenceNumber traffic. The worker-thread pair names remained absent.

evidence.title: Procmon boot-log review for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: A real Procmon boot-log lane completed cleanly on the RegProbe baseline, produced a PML plus CSV, and still returned zero filtered hits for AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads, AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads, and UuidSequenceNumber.

evidence.title: Shell-safe post-boot stress trigger for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: A shell-safe post-boot stress trigger lane combined service enumeration, event-log enumeration, CIM service export, and parallel service and file pressure on the clean RegProbe baseline. The lane produced a real PML plus CSV and still returned zero filtered hits for AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads, AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads, and UuidSequenceNumber.

evidence.title: ReactOS semantic hypothesis for the Executive worker-thread pair
evidence.summary: Official ReactOS source contains both Executive worker-thread value names, matching internal worker-thread variables, and code that clamps and adds the additional counts to delayed and critical worker-thread totals. The same source also places UuidSequenceNumber under the Rpc registry path, which helps separate that adjacent lane from the Executive pair.

decision.why: The Executive worker-thread pair now has a clean baseline export, a bounded boot-time ETL extract that proves early Session Manager\Executive activity from System (PID 4), exact current-build ntoskrnl string hits, current-build Ghidra fallback artifacts, and a tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up that produced exact RegQueryValue hits for both AdditionalCriticalWorkerThreads and AdditionalDelayedWorkerThreads under a concurrent burst trigger. That is enough for Class A within this project even though the lane remains research-only and not app-actionable.

open_questions: Can a cleaner symbol or binary target produce a higher-confidence semantic map than the current forced-boundary ntoskrnl fallback blocks?
How stable are these exact runtime reads across other Windows builds and non-VM environments?
Should this pair ever become a shipped tweak, or should it stay documentation-only even if semantics converge?

tested_on.notes: Baseline export ran on the visible-shell RegProbe baseline after the 2026-03-28 reboot-verified watchdog work. The bounded ETL review reused the successful boot trace from the same baseline, the Procmon boot-log follow-up completed cleanly on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable, the shell-safe post-boot stress trigger lane also completed cleanly on the same baseline, and the final exact-hit lightweight ETW follow-up ran on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330.
